subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Misinformation Effect and Memory (AAQ)
stimulus:
Introduction
This research study explores the effect of being presented with varying amounts of misinformation about a specific event on the memory of that event.
Participants
A total of 127 students at a large university were recruited through the university's research participation system. They participated in the study in exchange for course credit. The final sample of participants was 69% women and 31% men. The race/ethnicity of the participants was reported as follows: 65% Hispanic/Latino/a, 15% Black/African American, 11% White/European American, 4% Asian/Asian American, and 5% Other.1 The participants ranged in age from 18 to 39 years old (mean age = 21.21, standard deviation = 3.28).
Method
After providing consent, participants viewed a 6.5-minute silent video of a mock crime. The video depicted an electrician entering a house to fix items such as an oven and a light fixture. While he was in the house, he stole some of the owners' possessions. After watching the video, participants completed a 12-minute trivia questionnaire as a distractor task.2 Participants completed all portions of the study online.
Participants were then told that eight previous participants had written brief summaries of the robbery shown in the video. They were also told that one of those summaries would be presented to them, along with a profile of the previous participant, to refresh their memory. Unknown to the participants, these "previous participants" were not real people. The researchers created the previous participants' profiles based on the demographics of the population of students on the university campus. The goal was to create believable profiles so that the summaries would be more credible to the participants.
Participants were randomly assigned to one of three conditions to receive a summary with a specific amount of misinformation:
• Low misinformation condition: 8 of 40 sentences (20% misleading information)
• Medium misinformation condition: 20 of 40 sentences (50% misleading information)
• High misinformation condition: 32 of 40 sentences (80% misleading information)
The misleading information changed details about items seen in the video. No information was added. The non-misleading sentences were neutral to avoid strengthening the participant's memory of the target items. For example, a misleading item description was, "He noticed the dresser was covered with a brown cloth." In reality, the cloth was navy blue. The neutral description of that item was, "He noticed the dresser was covered with a cloth."
After reading an entire previous participant's summary, each current participant completed a 5-minute math test as a distractor task. Then, all participants were asked an identical set of 40 multiple-choice questions in random order about the mock crime video. Each question had three answer options:
1. Correct: the correct option corresponding to what was shown in the video (e.g., a navy blue cloth);
2. Misled: an incorrect answer that reflected misinformation provided in the summaries (e.g., a brown cloth); and
3. Incorrect: an incorrect answer containing information that was not portrayed in the video or any summary (e.g., a yellow cloth).
Participants then completed a questionnaire asking about the credibility of the previous participant's summary (e.g., "How good was the quality of the summary?" and "How familiar with the video was the participant who wrote this summary?").
Results and Discussion
The researchers analyzed the results of the questionnaire by comparing participants' correct responses with how participants viewed the credibility of the previous participant's summary. The researchers hypothesized that if participants distrusted the summary, they would be less likely to incorporate the information into their memory of the event, leading to their being less likely to choose an incorrect answer. The results are shown in the table.
Mean Percentages of Correct, Misled, and Incorrect Responses for Each Misinformation Condition
| Response Type | High Misinformation Group | Medium Misinformation Group | Low Misinformation Group |
|---|---|---|---|
| Correct | 63% | 66% | 74% |
| Misled | 30% | 27% | 19% |
| Incorrect | 7% | 7% | 7% |
The researchers found that within each condition, participants performed best if they distrusted the accuracy of the summary. Participants who were presented with higher levels of misinformation were more likely to report being distrustful, and those who reported being distrustful of the summary resisted more misinformation than those who did not.
Pena, M. M., Klemfuss, J. Z., Loftus, E. F., & Mindthoff, A. (2017). The effects of exposure to different amounts of misinformation and source credibility perception on source monitoring and memory accuracy. Psychology of Consciousness: Theory, Research, and Practice, 4(4), 337-347.
1: Language referencing racial, ethnic, or gender identities may be outdated or fail to reflect the complexities of identity that participants represent. Race/ethnicity categories reflect those reported in the original research article.
2: a task that is unrelated to the primary task that participants are asked to complete before they are asked to recall the target information.

parts.prompt: Identify the research method used in the study.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies the research method used in the study

required_elements: “The researchers used an experiment.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers used an experiment.”

required_elements: “The researchers included a questionnaire in their experiment.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers included a questionnaire in their experiment.”

required_elements: “Since students were randomly assigned to groups, this was
alternatives.correct_example: “Since students were randomly assigned to groups, this was

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately identifies the research method used in the study as an experiment. Responses that identify a correlational study or that merely describe the study topic (e.g., 'The researchers studied memory') do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the method as an experiment.

sample_responses.response: The researchers included a questionnaire in their experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point because 'experiment' is identified; the questionnaire detail does not negate the identification.

sample_responses.response: Since students were randomly assigned to groups, this was an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies random assignment as evidence of an experiment.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used a correlational study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; correlational study is incorrect — participants were randomly assigned to conditions, indicating an experiment.

sample_responses.response: The researchers studied memory.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes the topic rather than identifying the research method.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of high misinformation in the study.

scoring_points.description: States a measurable or quantifiable definition of the identified variable as used in the study

required_elements: “The researchers studied how much misinformation people can handle.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers studied how much misinformation people can handle.”

required_elements: “The researchers gave more misinformation to one group than another.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers gave more misinformation to one group than another.”

required_elements: “The researchers operationally defined high misinformation as 80% of the sentences in the narrative including incorrect information.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers operationally defined high misinformation as 80% of the sentences in the narrative including incorrect information.”

required_elements: “The researchers defined high misinformation as a story having 32 misleading sentences in it.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers defined high misinformation as a story having 32 misleading sentences in it.”

scoring_points.official_rubric: States that the operational definition of high misinformation is having 80% of the sentences in the narrative include incorrect information, or equivalently that high misinformation is defined as having 32 misleading sentences. A vague or non-quantifiable definition (e.g., 'gave more misinformation') does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers operationally defined high misinformation as 80% of the sentences in the narrative including incorrect information.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; provides the measurable, quantifiable definition of the high misinformation condition as stated in the study.

sample_responses.response: The researchers defined high misinformation as a story having 32 misleading sentences in it.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; 32 of 40 sentences is a measurable, quantifiable operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The researchers studied how much misinformation people can handle.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; describes the general research goal rather than stating a measurable operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The researchers gave more misinformation to one group than another.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; not a measurable or quantifiable definition of high misinformation specifically.

parts.prompt: Describe what the mean indicates for the percentage of correct responses between the high misinformation group and the low misinformation group.

scoring_points.description: Accurately describes what the identified statistic indicates in relation to the study

required_elements: “The mean of the high misinformation group is 63%.” [No
alternatives.correct_example: “The mean of the high misinformation group is 63%.” [No

required_elements: “The high misinformation group got less correct responses explanation of what that means in the study.]”
alternatives.correct_example: “The high misinformation group got less correct responses explanation of what that means in the study.]”

required_elements: “The differences in the means between the groups shows that the high misinformation group did not rely on the misinformation when the low misinformation group did.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The differences in the means between the groups shows that the high misinformation group did not rely on the misinformation when the low misinformation group did.”

required_elements: “The mean is the average of the responses in the study.” than the low misinformation group.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The mean is the average of the responses in the study.” than the low misinformation group.”

required_elements: “The group that got 80% misinformation relied on more misleading information than the group with 20% misinformation, since the mean averages of the high misinformation group was 63% compared to 74% for the low misinformation group.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The group that got 80% misinformation relied on more misleading information than the group with 20% misinformation, since the mean averages of the high misinformation group was 63% compared to 74% for the low misinformation group.”

required_elements: “The low misinformation group scored 11% higher in their correct responses than did the high misinformation group.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The low misinformation group scored 11% higher in their correct responses than did the high misinformation group.”

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately describes that exposure to a higher percentage of misinformation is associated with a lower mean percentage of correct responses. Simply restating the mean values without relating them to the study, or providing only a definition of 'mean,' does not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The high misinformation group got less correct responses than the low misinformation group.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; accurately describes the direction of the difference between the two group means in relation to the study.

sample_responses.response: The group that got 80% misinformation relied on more misleading information than the group with 20% misinformation, since the mean averages of the high misinformation group was 63% compared to 74% for the low misinformation group.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; uses specific data values and accurately explains what the difference in means indicates about the study's findings.

sample_responses.response: The low misinformation group scored 11% higher in their correct responses than did the high misinformation group.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; accurately describes the difference between the means in context of the study.

sample_responses.response: The mean of the high misinformation group is 63%.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; merely restates the mean value without explaining what it indicates in relation to the study.

sample_responses.response: The mean is the average of the responses in the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; provides only a definition of 'mean' without applying it to the study's findings.

parts.prompt: Identify at least one ethical guideline applied by the researchers.

scoring_points.description: Accurately identifies at least one ethical guideline applied by researchers in the study

required_elements: “It’s important to researchers to act ethically when
alternatives.correct_example: “It’s important to researchers to act ethically when

required_elements: “The researchers obtained informed consent from the conducting a study.” participants.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers obtained informed consent from the conducting a study.” participants.”

required_elements: “The researchers should have debriefed participants at the
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers should have debriefed participants at the

required_elements: “The article says that after the researchers obtained end of the study.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The article says that after the researchers obtained end of the study.”

required_elements: “The researchers protected the confidentiality of the participants in the study.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers protected the confidentiality of the participants in the study.”

required_elements: “The researchers gave students course credit when they participated in the study, which is unethical.” consent, the participants got to watch the video of the robbery.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The researchers gave students course credit when they participated in the study, which is unethical.” consent, the participants got to watch the video of the robbery.”

scoring_points.official_rubric: Accurately identifies that the researchers obtained informed consent. Responses that identify ethical guidelines not mentioned in the study (e.g., debriefing, confidentiality), guidelines that were not applied, or non-ethical-guideline study features do not earn this point.

sample_responses.response: The researchers obtained informed consent from the participants.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; informed consent is explicitly mentioned in the study — participants provided consent before beginning.

sample_responses.response: The article says that after the researchers obtained consent, the participants got to watch the video of the robbery.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; correctly identifies that informed consent was obtained and references the study for support.

sample_responses.response: The researchers should have debriefed participants at the end of the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; debriefing is not mentioned in the study, and the prompt asks for an ethical guideline that was applied, not one that should have been applied.

sample_responses.response: The researchers protected the confidentiality of the participants in the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; confidentiality is not identified in the study as an ethical guideline that was applied.

sample_responses.response: The researchers gave students course credit when they participated in the study, which is unethical.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; course credit for participation is not unethical, and this response misidentifies a feature of the study as an ethical violation.

parts.prompt: Explain the extent to which the research findings may or may not be generalizable using specific and relevant evidence from the study.
parts.scoring_notes: • If students assert the study is "partly" generalizable or asserts the study is both generalizable and not generalizable, their evidence must support their assertion by showing ways the study is generalizable and is not generalizable to earn the point.

scoring_points.description: Explains the extent to which the study is generalizable using specific and relevant evidence referencing participant variables from the study

required_elements: “The study used both men and women.” [The response does
alternatives.correct_example: “The study used both men and women.” [The response does

required_elements: “The study is generalizable to a larger population because not propose a claim about generalizability.] the participant sample was diverse.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The study is generalizable to a larger population because not propose a claim about generalizability.] the participant sample was diverse.”

required_elements: “The study is generalizable.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The study is generalizable.”

required_elements: “The study is generalizable to the larger population.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The study is generalizable to the larger population.”

required_elements: “The study is generalizable because the sample was large.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The study is generalizable because the sample was large.”

required_elements: “The study is not generalizable because they only used university students in their study.”
alternatives.correct_example: “The study is not generalizable because they only used university students in their study.”

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must both (1) propose a claim about generalizability referencing a population and (2) use specific, relevant evidence from participant information in the study. Responses that merely state 'The study is generalizable' without evidence, or that cite evidence without making a generalizability claim, do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The study is generalizable to a larger population because the participant sample was diverse.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; makes a claim about generalizability and supports it with specific participant evidence (racial/ethnic diversity of the sample).

sample_responses.response: The study is not generalizable because they only used university students in their study.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns the point; makes a claim about limited generalizability and supports it with specific participant information (university student sample).

sample_responses.response: The study used both men and women.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; provides participant evidence but does not propose a claim about generalizability.

sample_responses.response: The study is generalizable.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; makes a claim without referencing any specific participant evidence from the study.

sample_responses.response: The study is generalizable because the sample was large.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn the point; sample size is not a participant variable — it does not address the characteristics of participants that would impact generalizability.

parts.prompt: Explain how at least one of the research findings supports or refutes the misinformation effect.

scoring_points.description: Partial credit (1 pt): Uses evidence from the study but does not fully explain how the concept is supported or refuted, or explains without using a specific research finding.

required_elements: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how the psychological concept or hypothesis is supported or refuted.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how the misinformation effect is supported or refuted. OR explains that the misinformation effect is supported or refuted but does not use any specific results from the study.

sample_responses.response: The high misinformation group answered more questions using misleading information.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 1 point (evidence only); cites a result but does not explain how it supports or refutes the misinformation effect.

sample_responses.response: This study supports the misinformation effect, which is when people remember incorrect information that is presented to them.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 1 point (explanation without evidence); explains the concept but does not use a specific result from the study.

sample_responses.response: The study shows that the misinformation effect happens in the study.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn any points; does not explain how the findings support or refute the misinformation effect.

scoring_points.description: Full credit (2 pts): Uses a specific result from the study to accurately explain how the results support or refute the psychological concept, with correctly interpreted evidence.

required_elements: Uses the results of the study but does not explain how the psychological concept or hypothesis is supported or refuted.

common_traps: Must accurately interpret results — not just cite evidence
Must explicitly identify the psychological concept/theory from the AP Psychology CED
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: Uses at least one specific result from the study to accurately explain how the results support or refute the misinformation effect. The results must be accurately interpreted.

sample_responses.response: The study showed that the misinformation effect is supported because the data shows that participants in the high misinformation group were more likely to use the misleading information when answering questions (30%) than those in the low misinformation group (19%).
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; uses a specific result (30% vs. 19% misled responses) and accurately explains how it supports the misinformation effect.

sample_responses.response: This study provides evidence for the misinformation effect because it shows that when participants were exposed to a high level of misinformation, they were more likely to incorporate misleading information into their memories.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; accurately identifies the misinformation effect and uses the pattern of results to explain how it is supported.

sample_responses.response: One of the research findings that refutes the misinformation effect is that the participants performed best if they had distrusted the accuracy of the summary. The more that they were presented with misinformation, the more distrustful that they were, which meant that they had resisted more information in comparison to those who didn't receive as much misinformation.
sample_responses.commentary: Earns 2 points; correctly interprets the distrust finding as a refutation of the misinformation effect and supports the argument with accurate evidence from the study.

sample_responses.response: Since each group got 7% incorrect on the questionnaire, the misinformation effect is refuted.
sample_responses.commentary: Does not earn 2 points; incorrectly interprets the 7% 'incorrect' responses — this refers to non-misinformation errors, not a refutation of the misinformation effect.
